section.title: The Call of Wisdom and the Lure of Sin
section.sectionRationale: The first extended discourse of the book. A father's appeal to listen, verses 8 to 9, sets up a warning against violent men who entice the young, verses 10 to 19, and then Wisdom herself steps onto the street as a preacher, crying out in public and pronouncing judgment on those who refuse her, verses 20 to 33.
section.thesis: Two voices compete for the heart at the start of life: sinners who entice with quick, violent gain, and Wisdom who cries aloud in the open and warns that refusing her call has a real and lasting cost.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the parents' appeal to listen, verses 8 to 9; the enticement of violent men and its self destroying end, verses 10 to 19; and Wisdom's public call, refused, followed by the judgment that falls on those who refuse her, verses 20 to 33.

verses.text: Listen, my son, to your father's instruction, and do not forsake the teaching of your mother.
For they are a garland of grace on your head and a pendant around your neck.
My son, if {{chattaim|sinners}} entice you, do not yield to them.
If they say, "Come along, let us lie in wait for blood, let us ambush the innocent without cause,
let us swallow them alive like Sheol, and whole like those descending into the Pit.
We will find all manner of precious goods; we will fill our houses with plunder.
Throw in your lot with us; let us all share one purse."
My son, do not walk the road with them or set foot upon their path.
For their feet run to evil, and they are swift to shed blood.
How futile it is to spread the net where any bird can see it!
But they lie in wait for their own blood; they ambush their own lives.
Such is the fate of all who are greedy, whose unjust gain takes the lives of its possessors.

units.label: Wisdom's public call
units.note: she cries aloud, and is refused

verses.text: {{chokmah|Wisdom}} calls out in the street, she lifts her voice in the square;
in the main concourse she cries aloud, at the city gates she makes her speech:
"How long, O {{peti|simple ones}}, will you love your simple ways? How long will scoffers delight in their scorn and fools hate knowledge?
If you had repented at my rebuke, then surely I would have poured out my spirit on you; I would have made my words known to you.
Because you refused my call, and no one took my outstretched hand,
because you neglected all my counsel, and wanted none of my correction,
in turn I will mock your calamity; I will sneer when terror strikes you,
when your dread comes like a storm, and your destruction like a whirlwind, when distress and anguish overwhelm you.
Then they will call on me, but I will not answer; they will earnestly seek me, but will not find me.
For they hated knowledge and chose not to fear the LORD.
They accepted none of my counsel; they despised all my reproof.
So they will eat the fruit of their own way, and be filled with their own devices.
For the waywardness of the simple will slay them, and the complacency of fools will destroy them.
But whoever listens to me will dwell in safety, secure from the fear of evil."

chattaim.gloss: Sinners, those who habitually miss the mark of God's ways. Here they are organized and predatory, inviting the naive into violence and theft with the promise of shared plunder.

chokmah.gloss: Wisdom, personified in this passage as a woman who preaches publicly in the street and at the gates. She is not a private possession for the elite but a public voice offered to everyone who will listen.

peti.gloss: The simple or naive one, someone easily persuaded and without settled moral direction. Proverbs treats this state as dangerous but not hopeless, the simple can still turn and gain sense if they heed wisdom's call.

basic.context: Right after the book states its purpose, it shows that purpose in action. A father urges his child to listen to parental teaching, then warns against a gang of violent men who promise easy wealth through robbery and even murder. Then the passage shifts dramatically, Wisdom herself appears as a preacher in the public square, and when she is ignored, she promises that calamity will eventually come to those who refused her.
basic.hermeneutics: This is vivid, poetic warning literature, not a case study of one specific crime. The violent gang stands for any enticement to gain quickly through harming others, and Wisdom's public sermon stands for the many ordinary ways God's truth confronts us and is refused. The point is not that every sinner is a murderer, but that every temptation to sin promises gain and delivers ruin.
basic.originalLanguages: The chattaim, sinners, of verse 10 are not petty temptations but an organized invitation to violence, sharpening the warning. Wisdom, chokmah, is pictured as a public preacher, not a private secret. And those who refuse her are the peti, the simple, whose naivety becomes fatal only because they refuse correction, not because they were doomed from the start.
basic.typology: Wisdom's public cry that goes refused, followed by a judgment of being given over to one's own way, foreshadows the pattern Paul describes in Romans 1, God giving people over to what they insisted on choosing. It also anticipates Christ's own public preaching and weeping over a city that would not listen, Luke 19:41 to 44.
basic.christ: Wisdom stands in the street and cries out, offering herself freely, and is refused. Centuries later, Wisdom incarnate, Christ, stood in the temple courts and the streets of Jerusalem crying out the same offer, and wept over a city that would not come to Him, Matthew 23:37. He is both the wisdom that calls and, at the cross, the one who bears the judgment His hearers deserved for refusing.
basic.god: God is not hidden or stingy with wisdom; He sends it out publicly, urgently, repeatedly. Yet He is also just, He will not force anyone to listen forever, and He allows those who refuse Him to reap exactly what their own choices have sown.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 1:24 to 28 describes God giving people over to their own chosen path, echoing verses 31 to 32. Matthew 23:37 shows Christ crying over Jerusalem's refusal to be gathered. Luke 11:49 speaks of the wisdom of God sending prophets who are rejected. Galatians 6:7 states the same principle, a man reaps what he sows.
basic.oneStory: From the beginning, two calls compete for the human heart, the lure of quick and violent gain, and the public, patient call of wisdom. This passage's picture of a people who refuse wisdom until it is too late anticipates the whole Bible's story of a world that resists God's call, until Wisdom Himself, Jesus Christ, comes crying in the streets and finally dying so that those who turn to Him need never be turned away.

context.literary: The first of ten extended discourses that make up chapters 1 to 9. It moves from a domestic appeal, verses 8 to 9, into vivid dramatic speech, first the voice of the enticing sinners, then the voice of Wisdom herself personified as a street preacher, a literary technique used throughout these chapters to make abstract wisdom vivid and urgent.
context.historical: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom instruction was typically passed from father to son within the household; this passage keeps that family setting, verses 8 to 9, but widens wisdom's voice into the public square, matching the marketplaces and city gates where ancient Israelite public life and legal business actually happened.
context.canonical: This is the first appearance of Wisdom personified in Scripture, a figure developed further in Proverbs 8 and echoed in the New Testament's language of Christ as the wisdom of God. The picture of a call refused and judgment following also anticipates the prophets' repeated pattern of warning, refusal, and consequence.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To press home, through vivid contrasting speeches, that every young person faces a real choice between violent, quick gain and wisdom's patient call, and that refusing wisdom's call carries a real and eventually inescapable cost.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The command to listen to parents and refuse violent enticement is straightforwardly prescriptive. But Wisdom's closing speech, verses 20 to 33, is best read as a description of a general pattern, persistent refusal of God's wisdom tends toward a hardened, self chosen ruin, rather than a promise that calamity strikes on a fixed timetable for every individual sinner.

debates.question: Does verse 28, 'they will call on me, but I will not answer,' describe God as unwilling to forgive?

views.label: A picture of hardened, final refusal
views.case: The passage describes people who persistently despised correction, verses 24 to 25and 30; the 'calling' in verse 28 pictures a desperate, too late cry for rescue from consequences, not a sincere turning to God in repentance, which Scripture elsewhere always meets with grace.

views.label: A sober warning against presuming on future repentance
views.case: The verse functions as a warning, not a metaphysical claim about God's willingness to forgive; it tells the naive that delaying wisdom indefinitely is dangerous, since some consequences of sin cannot be undone even after a change of heart, as with Esau's tears in Hebrews 12:17.

debates.bearing: Either way, this is a warning against presuming there is always a comfortable later moment to turn to wisdom; it does not contradict the free offer of grace to genuine repentance found throughout Scripture, but it does deny that refusal is ever safe or consequence free.

study.originalLanguages: The chattaim, sinners, of verse 10 propose an organized, violent scheme, sharpening the danger of the enticement. Wisdom, chokmah, cries out publicly rather than whispering privately, showing that wisdom is offered, not hoarded. Those who refuse her are named peti, simple, a state Proverbs treats as dangerous precisely because it is curable, correction was offered and refused, not because such people were beyond hope from the start.

typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Wisdom of God, preaching openly in Jerusalem and weeping over the city's refusal, Matthew 23:37, Luke 19:41-44.

typology.type: Being given over to one's own way, Proverbs 1:31
typology.fulfillment: God's judicial giving over of persistent sinners to their own chosen path, Romans 1:24-28.

study.christ: Wisdom stands in the open and calls, and is refused, and then warns that the refusers will one day call and not be answered. That whole scene finds its deepest echo in Jesus, the wisdom of God incarnate, who taught publicly in the temple courts and streets, and who wept over Jerusalem because it would not gather to Him as a hen gathers her chicks, Matthew 23:37. Yet where Wisdom's speech in Proverbs ends in warning, Christ's public ministry ends at the cross, where He bears the judgment that refusal deserves, so that everyone who does turn to Him, even now, is never turned away, John 6:37.
study.god: God does not hide wisdom from anyone; He sends it into the street, the square, and the gate, the most public places of ancient life. Yet He is also patient only so long; those who despise correction again and again will finally be left to the very path they chose, a real and sobering justice beneath His generosity.

crossReferences.note: God gives persistent sinners over to their own chosen way, echoing the judgment described in verses 31 to 32.
Christ cries over Jerusalem's refusal to be gathered, echoing Wisdom's public call and rejection.
Wisdom's public cry is repeated and developed further in the next major discourse.
A man reaps what he sows, the same principle behind the self destroying end of the greedy in verse 19.

study.oneStory: From its first discourse, Proverbs sets two voices before every reader, violent men offering quick plunder, and Wisdom crying openly for anyone who will listen. Those who refuse her find their own way turns against them. That pattern of a patient, public call met with refusal runs through the prophets and reaches its climax in Christ, Wisdom in the flesh, who still calls in the open today and still will not turn away anyone who comes.

quiz.q: What do the violent men in verses 10 to 14 promise, and what do they actually deliver?

options.text: They promise quick, shared plunder, but their own scheme ends up destroying them.
options.note: Verses 18 to 19 make clear their violence recoils on themselves.

options.text: They promise wealth and deliver it without any cost.
options.note: The passage explicitly says their own greed takes their lives, verse 19.

options.text: They promise friendship, which they genuinely provide.
options.note: Their appeal to shared partnership, verse 14, is a cover for violence, not real friendship.

options.text: They promise wisdom in exchange for loyalty.
options.note: They promise material gain, not wisdom; this confuses their offer with what Wisdom herself offers later in the passage.

quiz.q: Where and how does Wisdom call out in verses 20 to 21?

options.text: Publicly, in the street, the square, and at the city gates.
options.note: The passage stresses that Wisdom's call is public and open to all, not hidden or exclusive.

options.text: Privately, only to a select few disciples.
options.note: The text emphasizes public, open proclamation, the opposite of a private, exclusive call.

options.text: Only through written scrolls circulated among scribes.
options.note: The passage pictures a spoken, public cry, not a written document.

options.text: Mainly inside the royal court.
options.note: The gate could include official business, but the emphasis is broader, the street and square, ordinary public life, not the court alone.

quiz.q: What happens to those who refuse Wisdom's repeated call, according to verses 24 to 31?

options.text: They are eventually left to reap the fruit of their own chosen way when calamity comes.
options.note: Verses 31 to 32 state this directly, they eat the fruit of their own way.

options.text: Wisdom keeps calling forever regardless of their response.
options.note: The passage instead describes a real turning point after persistent refusal, not endless, consequence free calling.

options.text: They are immediately destroyed the moment they first ignore her.
options.note: The passage describes a pattern of repeated refusal, verses 24 to 25, not instant destruction after one lapse.

options.text: Nothing changes for them either way.
options.note: The passage is explicit that real consequences follow persistent refusal.

quiz.q: What does the word peti, simple, describe in this passage?

options.text: One who is naive and easily led, a state that is dangerous but can still be corrected by heeding wisdom.
options.note: Verse 22 rebukes the simple for loving their simple ways, implying a choice, not a fixed condition.

options.text: Someone who is intellectually disabled and therefore blameless.
options.note: The term describes a moral and volitional state that Proverbs holds people responsible for, not a lack of capacity.

options.text: A title reserved only for children under a certain age.
options.note: The term applies broadly to anyone easily persuaded, not a strict age category.

options.text: Someone already fully wise but pretending otherwise.
options.note: This inverts the meaning; the simple genuinely lack settled wisdom, they are not wise people in disguise.

quiz.q: How does this passage anticipate Christ?

options.text: Wisdom's public cry and its refusal foreshadow Christ's open teaching and His weeping over Jerusalem's refusal.
options.note: Matthew 23:37 and Luke 19:41 to 44 draw exactly this parallel of public offer and tragic refusal.

options.text: It has no connection to the New Testament at all.
options.note: The New Testament itself draws directly on this pattern of a public call refused.

options.text: It shows Christ only as a stern judge with no patience.
options.note: The passage, like Christ's ministry, actually shows patient, repeated appeal before any judgment falls.

options.text: It teaches that wisdom is only for a spiritual elite.
options.note: The opposite is true, Wisdom cries in the open, offered to everyone who will listen, not an elite few.

questions: The violent men in this passage promise quick gain through harming others. What shortcuts are you tempted toward that promise a fast reward but would really cost you or someone else dearly?

Wisdom cries out in the open, in ordinary public places, not in secret. Where in your ordinary week is God's truth calling out to you plainly, whether through Scripture, a sermon, or a friend's honest word?
The passage warns against repeated refusal hardening into ruin. Is there a correction you have been putting off responding to, hoping to deal with it later?
Verse 33 promises safety and rest to whoever listens to wisdom. What would change in your sense of security this week if you truly believed that promise?
Wisdom's call anticipates Christ's own call, and His tears over those who refuse Him. How does knowing that Jesus still calls patiently change the way you want to respond to Him today?

translationNotes.note: Some manuscript traditions of Proverbs omit verse 16 as a later addition drawn from Isaiah 59:7, but its sense fits the surrounding warning and is retained in most translations, including the BSB.
The main concourse can also be rendered from the top of the wall, a reference to a public place where speakers could be heard by a crowd.
The Hebrew term rendered fool in this chapter denotes one who is morally deficient by settled choice, not merely lacking in intelligence.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Proverbs 1:8-33. One em dash present in the source at verse 14 was rewritten as a closing period to comply with the no dash rule; the wording is otherwise unchanged and complete.
